text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Saturated fat makes up 33 percent of the fat in an egg","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Saturated fat ( palmitic, stearic, and myristic acids) makes up 33 percent of the fat in an egg;","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly states the proportion of saturated fat in egg fat.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"33 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Monounsaturated fat makes up 38 percent of the fat in an egg","claimType":"empirical","quote":"monounsaturated fat makes up 38 percent;","entailment":"The quoted phrase explicitly gives the percentage of monounsaturated fat in egg fat.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"38 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Polyunsaturated fat makes up 20 percent of the fat in an egg","claimType":"empirical","quote":"polyunsaturated makes up 20 percent;","entailment":"The quoted text directly reports the proportion of polyunsaturated fat in egg fat.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"20 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Cholesterol makes up 4 percent of the fat in an egg","claimType":"empirical","quote":"cholesterol makes up 4 percent.[70]","entailment":"The quoted sentence states the percentage of cholesterol in egg fat.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"4 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Egg white consists primarily of water (88 percent) and protein (11 percent), with no cholesterol and 0.2 percent fat","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The egg white consists primarily of water (88 percent) and protein (11 percent), with no cholesterol and 0.2 percent fat.[71]","entailment":"The quoted sentence provides the composition percentages of egg white.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"water 88 %, protein 11 %, cholesterol 0 %, fat 0.2 %","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Some research suggests dietary cholesterol increases the ratio of total to HDL cholesterol and therefore adversely affects the body's cholesterol profile","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Some research suggests dietary cholesterol increases the ratio of total to HDL cholesterol and, therefore, adversely affects the body's cholesterol profile;[72]","entailment":"The quoted clause reports a suggested effect of dietary cholesterol on cholesterol ratios and health profile.","population":"","intervention":"dietary cholesterol","comparator":"","outcome":"ratio of total to HDL cholesterol, adverse cholesterol profile","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Other studies show that moderate consumption of eggs, up to one a day, does not appear to increase heart disease risk in healthy individuals","claimType":"empirical","quote":"whereas other studies show that moderate consumption of eggs, up to one a day, does not appear to increase heart disease risk in healthy individuals.[73]","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports an observed lack of increased heart disease risk with moderate egg consumption in healthy people.","population":"healthy individuals","intervention":"moderate egg consumption (up to one a day)","comparator":"","outcome":"heart disease risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Harold McGee argues that the cholesterol in the egg yolk is not what causes a problem, because fat (particularly saturated fat) is much more likely to raise cholesterol levels than the consumption of cholesterol","claimType":"value","quote":"Harold McGee argues that the cholesterol in the egg yolk is not what causes a problem, because fat (particularly saturated fat) is much more likely to raise cholesterol levels than the consumption of cholesterol.[20]","entailment":"The quoted sentence conveys McGee's opinion that fat, not yolk cholesterol, is the primary driver of cholesterol level changes.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"normative"},{"statement":"A meta-analysis from 2013 found that eating four eggs per week was associated with a 29 percent increase in the relative risk of developing diabetes","claimType":"empirical","quote":"A meta-analysis from 2013 found that eating four eggs per week was associated with a 29 percent increase in the relative risk of developing diabetes.[74]","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports the quantified relative risk increase linked to a specific egg consumption level.","population":"","intervention":"eating four eggs per week","comparator":"","outcome":"relative risk of developing diabetes","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"29 percent increase","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"A 2020 meta-analysis found that there was no overall association between moderate egg consumption and risk of type 2 diabetes","claimType":"empirical","quote":"A 2020 meta-analysis found that there was no overall association between moderate egg consumption and risk of type 2 diabetes and that the risk found in US studies was not found in European or Asian studies.[77]","entailment":"The quoted sentence states the lack of overall association between moderate egg intake and type 2 diabetes risk.","population":"","intervention":"moderate egg consumption","comparator":"","outcome":"risk of type 2 diabetes","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"no overall association","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"A 2015 meta-analysis found an association between higher egg consumption (five a week) with increased risk of breast cancer compared to no egg consumption","claimType":"empirical","quote":"A 2015 meta-analysis found an association between higher egg consumption (five a week) with increased risk of breast cancer compared to no egg consumption.[78]","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports a link between a specified egg consumption level and higher breast cancer risk versus no consumption.","population":"","intervention":"higher egg consumption (five a week)","comparator":"no egg consumption","outcome":"breast cancer risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"increased risk","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"A 2021 review did not find a significant association between egg consumption and breast cancer","claimType":"empirical","quote":"A 2021 review did not find a significant association between egg consumption and breast cancer.[81]","entailment":"The quoted sentence indicates the absence of a significant link between egg intake and breast cancer.","population":"","intervention":"egg consumption","comparator":"","outcome":"breast cancer","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"no significant association","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"One systematic review and meta-analysis of egg consumption found that higher consumption of eggs (more than 1 egg/day) was associated with a significant reduction in risk of coronary artery disease","claimType":"empirical","quote":"One systematic review and meta-analysis of egg consumption found that higher consumption of eggs (more than 1 egg/day) was associated with a significant reduction in risk of coronary artery disease.[83]","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports a significant risk reduction for coronary artery disease with higher egg consumption.","population":"","intervention":"higher egg consumption (more than 1 egg/day)","comparator":"","outcome":"risk of coronary artery disease","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"significant reduction","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Another systematic review and meta-analysis of dietary cholesterol and egg consumption found that egg consumption was associated with an increased all-cause mortality and CVD mortality","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Another systematic review and meta-analysis of dietary cholesterol and egg consumption found that egg consumption was associated with an increased all-cause mortality and CVD mortality.[84]","entailment":"The quoted sentence links egg consumption with higher all-cause and cardiovascular disease mortality.","population":"","intervention":"egg consumption","comparator":"","outcome":"all-cause mortality and CVD mortality","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"increased","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"A 2018 meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials found that consumption of eggs increases total cholesterol (TC), LDL-C and HDL-C compared to no egg-consumption, but not to low-egg control diets","claimType":"empirical","quote":"A 2018 meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials found that consumption of eggs increases total cholesterol (TC), LDL-C and HDL-C compared to no egg-consumption, but not to low-egg control diets.[86]","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports that egg consumption raises TC, LDL-C, and HDL-C relative to no egg intake.","population":"","intervention":"consumption of eggs","comparator":"no egg-consumption","outcome":"total cholesterol, LDL-C, HDL-C","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"increases","modality":"causal"}]}
